questions.prompt: Which best describes what Huntress is?

choices.label: An anti-virus product that runs alongside Defender.
choices.rationale: Conflates Huntress with AV. AV decides whether to block; Huntress collects telemetry, the SOC analyses, and the tech actions a recommendation. They are designed to run together.

choices: A managed security platform combining endpoint, identity, and optional SIEM telemetry with a 24/7 human-and-AI SOC.

choices.label: A standalone EDR product sold mainly to enterprise security teams.
choices.rationale: Wrong on two counts. Huntress isn't standalone EDR — it includes ITDR and optional SIEM under one platform — and its target market is mostly MSPs.

choices.label: A vulnerability scanner that produces remediation tickets.
choices.rationale: Vulnerability scanning isn't a Huntress surface. Telemetry-driven incident response is.

questions.explanation: Three current product surfaces (EDR, ITDR, optional SIEM), plus ransomware canaries under EDR, and a SOC that produces Incident Reports with recommendations.

questions.prompt: A new tech says: "Huntress is basically AV with a better dashboard." Which correction best captures why that's the wrong mental model?

choices.label: Huntress is more expensive than AV, so it must be better.
choices.rationale: Price doesn't describe the model. The new tech will still treat Huntress like AV unless the SOC and recommendation flow get named.

choices: AV blocks autonomously based on signatures; Huntress collects telemetry, a SOC analyst writes a recommendation, and the tech actions it.

choices.label: Huntress doesn't need an agent on the endpoint.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts. Managed EDR runs an agent on Windows, macOS, and Linux endpoints.

choices.label: Huntress only works on servers, not workstations.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts. Endpoint coverage includes workstations and servers across the listed OSes.

questions.explanation: The three differences — notify vs. block, graded vs. binary, named analyst vs. signatures — all hang off this core distinction.

questions.prompt: A peer says: "If Huntress raised a detection, the AV must have failed." Why is that mental model wrong?

choices.label: AV never fails — it catches everything by definition.
choices.rationale: Wrong on its own terms. AV does miss things. But the deeper issue is that AV and Huntress watch different things, so both being involved isn't a failure of either.

choices: AV may have caught and quarantined the file; Huntress reports on the behaviour around it (autoruns, scheduled tasks, lateral signals). Both being involved is the design, not a failure.

choices.label: Huntress only raises detections when AV is disabled.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts. Huntress runs whether AV is active or not.

choices.label: AV runs on the network only; Huntress runs on the endpoint.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the architecture. AV runs on the endpoint too. The difference is what each watches, not where it lives.

questions.explanation: The two products watch different layers — AV blocks files, Huntress watches the behaviour around them.

questions.prompt: "Huntress will block things for me." What's the right correction?

choices.label: Correct — once you enable auto-block in settings, Huntress acts on every incident.
choices.rationale: There is no general auto-block setting. The default model is collect, analyse, recommend, action — the tech is the actioning party.

choices: Mostly no — the default model is collect, analyse, recommend, action. ITDR has some auto-response actions, but EDR generally hands you a recommendation to execute.

choices.label: Wrong — Huntress never takes any action automatically under any circumstance.
choices.rationale: Too absolute. ITDR has documented auto-response actions (lesson 15).

choices.label: Correct — that's the whole reason MSPs buy Huntress, to offload action from the helpdesk.
choices.rationale: Misreads what the SOC offloads. The SOC offloads triage and recommendation, not the action itself.

questions.explanation: ITDR auto-response (covered in lesson 15) is the named exception. EDR is recommendation-driven.

questions.prompt: Huntress is designed to sit alongside which of the following in a typical MSP stack?
choices: An AV/EPP product, an RMM, and a PSA.

choices.label: Replaces the AV product entirely; the RMM and PSA still run.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Huntress and AV are explicitly designed to run together.

choices.label: Replaces all three — AV, RMM, and PSA — once enabled.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Huntress doesn't do RMM deployment/patching or ticketing.

choices.label: Sits inside the AV product as a plugin.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the architecture. Huntress is a separate platform with its own agents and portal.

questions.explanation: Huntress catches what the others miss and triages the catch before it reaches the tech.

questions.prompt: Which is the easiest tell that you're looking at a Huntress communication rather than an AV-product alert?

choices.label: It mentions a hostname.
choices.rationale: AV alerts include hostnames too. Hostname alone isn't a Huntress tell.

choices: The notification is signed by a named SOC analyst.

choices.label: It uses the word "malware".
choices.rationale: Both AV alerts and Huntress reports can use the word. Vocabulary alone won't tell you which platform you're looking at.

choices.label: It includes a severity colour.
choices.rationale: AV products also use severity colours. The named-analyst signature is the Huntress-specific marker.

questions.explanation: The named-analyst signature is the easiest recognition tell — AV alerts don't have a human signature.

questions.prompt: The Huntress Managed EDR agent runs on which operating systems?

choices.label: Windows only.
choices.rationale: Incomplete. The lesson names Windows, macOS, and Linux.

choices.label: Windows and macOS.
choices.rationale: Incomplete. Linux is also covered.

choices.label: Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android.
choices.rationale: Mobile OSes aren't EDR endpoints. ITDR covers mobile sign-in events via the identity surface, but the agent itself is desktop/server.

questions.explanation: Endpoint coverage spans the three named desktop/server OSes.

questions.prompt: Managed SAT (Security Awareness Training) is one of the four core product surfaces this course covers.
questions.explanation: False. SAT is a separate Huntress product and is deliberately out of scope for this course. The four surfaces covered are EDR, ITDR, SIEM, and ransomware canaries (under EDR).

questions.prompt: A senior overhears you talking to a peer about Huntress and says: "Just remember, working a Huntress ticket is responding to an analyst, not responding to an alert." Why is that distinction important?
choices: Because the SOC analyst's recommendation is the work; the alert is the analyst's input, not yours.

choices.label: Because SOC analysts are senior staff and you should never disagree with their dispositions.
choices.rationale: Misses the point and overstates it. The distinction is about workflow shape, not rank. Clarifying questions are welcomed; unilateral overrides are the problem.

choices.label: Because alerts in Huntress are unreliable and have to be filtered through the analyst.
choices.rationale: Wrong framing. Alerts aren't unreliable; they're raw telemetry. The analyst's job is to turn raw telemetry into an actionable recommendation.

choices.label: Because alerts cost extra; only the analyst-reviewed incidents are included in the subscription.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the commercial model. The analyst-reviewed flow is the product.

questions.explanation: The lesson states "the work the tech does on Huntress is mostly responding to an analyst, not responding to an alert" as the shape every later lesson depends on.
